speaker: interviewer
text: No it's six.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could I take a minute?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you're hooked up. Hold on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oops. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I-- I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six, January 12, 1995, interview with Bernard Gotfryd.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to take it all out, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, That's fine. Thank you. [LAUGHS] [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'll be right back. OK. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did I slate already?

speaker: interviewee
text: You slated already.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you've-- you married someone who was also from your hometown, Gina. Although you didn't meet her until you came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: As it turned out, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As it turned out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't meet her, but I saw her. Would you like me to tell you how it happened? Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Eh, briefly, yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this was in the small ghetto. I was waiting, one morning, to be escorted to the studio. I was waiting for the policeman to escort us. And right across from where I was standing was a courtyard, an apartment building.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was this little girl in a gray coat, a beret, and two braids, sort of on the blonde side, light, standing right in the middle of the courtyard, which was maybe 50 feet away from where I was, looking up at a window-- as if she was waiting for somebody. And when I saw her, I thought to myself, God! What is she doing in the ghetto with such looks like she has? She should be somewhere hiding and hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had-- she didn't look Jewish at all. That was her. When I saw her again in 1950, in-- in New York City, this is what I recognized [? then. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in 1952. I'm bad in mathematics. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And I know you had two children and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lost-- unfortunately, we lost our daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a grandson?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a married son who is-- who has a little boy of-- who is going to be six. Elias is his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wrote your book, 'Anton the Dove Fancier--'

speaker: interviewee
text: --Fancier'.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in 1990, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The book was published in 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: But I wrote it-- it took me six years, part-time job. Vacations, lunch time, traveling, that's how I wrote it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what awards have you won? Has the book won for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it got the Christopher. And it got the Martha Albrand PEN. And some nominations-- several nominations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The stage rights were sold to England. Yes, I forgot about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We've covered so much ground, and you're-- you're so articulate. And obviously everything you've experienced is still with you. Is there any image, maybe, that we haven't discussed or anything that you'd like to finish this tape with, about how your experiences have shaped you? Or why you're doing this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure there's a lot-- I'm sure that there's a lot that we could still talk about. But I'm afraid to get into it, because it would never end. This is an experience that practically, in my mind, never ends. It stays with you. You live with it. It just never leaves you. The more you think about it, the more you remember. Memory is very tricky. It's a tricky thing. Many times you feel that you could get it out of your system. I thought that-- I thought that that's what would happen after I wrote that book. Finally, I got it out of my system. But this is not how it works. You just told your story. But the story is still in your system, and more. And it haunts you. And I think that's how it goes. I don't think there's any other way of dealing with it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Because there are-- there's so much more that I wanted to write about but that I haven't. Because for the simple reason that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --maybe episodes I wanted to write were not I'm not that significant. I don't know. But for some reason, when I-- I discussed this book with my editor at the publishers, they felt that this would be quite, well, sufficient, what I did. And still I feel that-- nowadays, I still feel that I-- that the book is-- that the book hasn't finish. That I hadn't finished it right-- that there is so much more to it that I missed. This sort of, at times, bothers me. Because I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It gives me a sense of guilt that I left out so many other episodes about so many other people who deserve to-- to be remembered and written about. But then again, to write another book about those experiences would be a little too-- emotionally too-- too much to bear. I remember, specifically, one story which I wrote over a weekend. The last morning-- this was the time when I saw my mother for the last time. That was probably the most difficult time, emotionally, for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was really-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't think I could finish that story. But that sat with me for 38 years. It was constantly on my mind. I really thought about that episode. Day in, day out, it haunted me. And then writing it was just a-- I got a-- a traumatic experience. That story, specifically, more so than the others. And now, for me to think about another book is almost impossible. I don't think I could handle it. I don't think I could cope with the emotions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for doing this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. Are you ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandmother Esther Gotfryd, whom I photographed with a borrowed camera in 1938, out in the country. She was a very cheerful, eternally-smiling lady with a big heart. It comes right through her eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What you can do is you can do the photo switches each time, so it can move a little quicker. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is supposed to be my grandfather, Abraham Gotfryd. Since most of our photographs got destroyed and lost during the war, we never had a photograph of grandpa.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day my uncle was looking at some paper, a newspaper, and he came across a centennial issue of a new stamp of Dr. Freud. And he said, God, looks just like my father. And sure enough, this is what happened. And we had it copied. And that was a handmade portrait. And this is the reproduction of Dr. Freud, who looks just like my grandfather. So we all think this is grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my father taken in 1940, under the occupation, and for his ID card, the only photo we have of him. He got executed by the Nazis during a march in 1944. They-- when they evacuated the [INAUDIBLE] labor camp in Radom, on the way to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just describe-- and let's just start describing what the whole thing is.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These are photographs of my sister, Hanka, Helen. The first on the left, top left, is of her when she was still a high-school student in Poland. Next picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --to her right is-- to our right, is 1945, after she came out of camps. This was the picture taken in Radom, Poland, when she came to look for us, my brother and myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are two photographs of her with the-- in the woods, where she's picking flowers. These two I-- I shot in 1946, in Stuttgart, where we stayed, in this DP camp. The woods were right near us where we used to live. Now, directly to the left is a little portrait of her, which I took of her in this country, in New York. This was done in 1948. And the lower left, the lowest left, is after she got married. She gave birth to her daughter, Shelly, as a baby, as an infant. And next to-- to her-- to our right, to her left, are two photographs of Shelly. She was about 11 and maybe 14. [PAUSES FOR 64 SECONDS] This is a photograph of my Aunt [? Diana's ?] wedding party, at my grandparents' house. This was exactly May the 5th, 1935. I'm right there in the-- in the photograph. I was 11 years old, next to my Aunt [INAUDIBLE] and my favorite cousin, Mietek, on our left. And I remember when we were coming home very early morning. Must've been like, four o'clock in the morning. It snowed. And it was quite a-- a revelation, in May, to snow. And I remember trying to gather enough to make a snow ball, as a symbolic gesture, but I wasn't able-- I-- I wasn't able to succeed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- there was not enough, you know, snow to make a decent snowball. So the little one that I gathered up, I just threw away in-- disgust, saying, oh, what kind of a snow is it any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, my Aunt [? Diana ?] is the only one who's alive. She's 85 and she lives in Florida. And she's on the extreme lower left, at the table. This-- this is where she was. I do have a picture of her, Sherry? And-- a beautiful woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can see from here that she's beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's 85 now. She's still-- she's still beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. You said four--

speaker: interviewer
text: One sec. Can you start again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of all the people in this picture, there are four people who are still alive. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] One in Israel, one in Australia, and two in this country. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This happens to be the first picture I ever took with a baby Brownie, which I got from my father. I could have been 11 or 12 years old at the time. These are rooftops in our neighborhood. I took it from a balcony at my aunt's place. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS] This picture is 60 years old. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] We have a picture of myself taken in 1940, for the German Kennkarte, which is the ID. My sister was able to retrieve this little picture from 1945, after the war, when she came to Poland. She went to the city hall and went through all the files. And, sure enough, she found certain photographs of the family. This is what she did. She just took it off-- took it with her. And this is how it came back. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself again, 1945, probably in early October, Radom, Poland. This was a time when I went back to look for Hanka, my sister, who was looking for us at the same time. Eventually I found her some weeks later. And the picture was taken by a friend of mine, whom I accidentally met in Radom, a young lady who had a camera. And she thought that's a good idea to go to the park and take a picture of each other. And I took a picture of her. She took a picture of me. And that's what it is. October, '45. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a photograph of my Uncle Hershel and his bride, Annette, with whose smile I was totally infatuated with when I must have been seven or eight years old. We had a picture like this back in Radom, Poland. And as a result, I wrote a story about this picture. And eventually, after the war is over, I met them in Paris, France. This is a photograph that Aunt Annette gave me when I visited her in France, in 1985. She didn't live, any longer, in Paris. She moved down South. And one morning, accidentally, she just said, let me look up some old photographs. You'll like pictures. And, sure enough, right on top of the pile sat this picture. And I said, I must have this. And she said, well, take it. It's yours. Here it is. Uncle Hershel and Aunt Annette.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why is it damaged?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I asked her this question. And she said, OK. During the war, when they were separated, she was masquerading under forged papers. And she had this picture. This picture turned out to be too big for her pocketbook, so she folded it in half. And this is how she was able to keep it in her pocketbook. And this is how it came out. Of course, she-- we scotch taped it. And I had it copied later. And that's the result. This is a photograph of Uncle Hershel, Uncle Ignatz, his brother, and myself in Paris, early in 1946, on one of my visits. They wanted very much to have a picture with me. And sure enough, we went to a studio. And here we are. Uncle Ignatz was his brother who spent-- my uncle, another uncle whom I never knew. I met him for the first time-- who spent 16 years in the Foreign Legion. He actually came in with the French army to liberate Paris in 1944. And he, too, accidentally found his brother, Hershel. And this is how it all came about. Uncle Ignatz eventually emigrated to Israel. And this is where he died in the mid '70s. He was a real feisty man, always fighting, always fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture of myself in-- in the service, obviously. I was a photographer with the Signal Corps. And this is one of my assignments. A friend of mine just decided to take a picture of me. He felt that I am never been photographed. So he took pity on me and took that photograph. That's about the only good picture I have of myself in the service. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is the apartment building, which belonged to my Grandfather Gotfryd, in Radom, Poland, where we used to live. Well, some of our relatives lived there too. Our apartment is on the very lower left, the three windows that one can see facing us. And there were three more windows around the corner, which we cannot see. This is where I lived most of my childhood. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture, by the way, I took in 1983, when I went back to Poland-- excuse me-- on an assignment for 'Newsweek' magazine, when I traveled with Pope Paul-- Paul John. This is a view of Radom, a Main Street in Radom. This is called [INAUDIBLE] Street, looking north. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, this picture was taken in 1983 in June, when I traveled with the Pope. I was able to go back to Radom for several hours. That is all I could-- I was able to spend. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of the entrance to my father's store in Radom, on Main Street. Which I photographed in 1983. And at that time there was a watchmaker. At some point I wanted to see what it was like inside. I stuck my head in and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the man just looked at me. And I said to him, this used to be my father's shop, once upon a time. And he said to me, well, he said, I'm sorry about that. But now it's my shop. [LAUGHS] And that was it. [PAUSES FOR 34 SECONDS] This is another photograph of Radom, Poland. This is the building where my grandparents used to live. And this is the staircase leading to the entrance to the kitchen. Right above the staircase is the balcony. And the windows on the balcony are of the living room. And from the balcony was an entrance, another staircase going up to my grandfather's attic, which was one of the most mysterious places I ever encountered. But that would be a long story to talk about it. So I'll leave it for some other time. [PAUSES FOR 35 SECONDS] This is my book I've written. A collection of 21 short pieces, of course, dealing with the Holocaust, my personal experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the award?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, next to it, of course, is the Christopher's. The book won the Christopher and the PEN, Martha Albrand citation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to explain exactly what the Christopher Award is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um-- God, I knew the whole story. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe we can just actually show them the medal on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I forgot the whole idea behind the award. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I should know, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]
